words: SAM SCHRAGER: How you happened to come to Moscow in the first place?
HARRY SAMPSON: You mean what I just told you? Well, our for' coming to
Moscow was a neighbor of ours that had a couple of years before here.
And he wrote my father that there was an opening as a meat cutter with
Hagan, Cushing and Polk, tot we packed up, took the Northern Pacific and
arrived in Moscow in March  wet, rainy day. It was muddy and miserable.
But we were able to stay over night with the people told us about the
position and then the next day we found a place to rent on Third and
Jackson across the street from the old Pleasant Home Boarding House. Rented
the house for twelve dollars a month until we could build a house of our
own. Moscow was kind of divided into a special section for the Scandinavian
people, and it was called Swede town. And of course. being Scandinavian,
looked for a place up there. Well, you couldn't buy a lot, you had to
buy an acre. We bought an acre and built a house on it—three bedroom house
for twelve hundred dollars and the ground was two hundted dollars. The
neighbor next door was a contractor; he built the house-- Gustav Johnson
andpis son, Theodore. In order to get the place landscaped and fixed up he
got Allen Romstead, whose father Victor Romstead, to plant thirty-two
fruit trees of his own choice on the place. And then a barn was built
where we kept a cow, chickens and a pig. And remember this was only five
blocks from Main Street, so it wasnt very far from town,and you could do
those things in those days. We had to have an outhouse because there
were no sewer facilities and we had to dig our own well for our own
water. And that lasted until about 1910 or '12 before we got
sewer connected to the place.

speaker: SAM
words: Where would that be today, about what street?

speaker: HS
words: Corner of Logan and Seventh. Then in 1905, I think it was, we moved out to

speaker: HS
words: the packing plant, Hagan and Cushing Packing Plant where father could

speaker: HS
words: traftfer from a meat cutter to a butcher. And we lived there, he could make

speaker: HS
words: more money because he got a free house, free milk, free meat, free fuel,

speaker: HS
words: free everything. All he had to buy were a few of the necessities ife Wear

speaker: HS
words: and so on. Well, from there, we there for about two yars and then

speaker: HS
words: we moved to towft ^e passed away in 1907.

speaker: SAM
words: You were growing up right during that time, you were just a boy.

speaker: HS
words: Well, I was nine years old when I came to Moscow so I remember a good deal

speaker: HS
words: of the early history of Moscow.

speaker: SAM
words: Do you remember about how you played as a boy, the kinds of things that

speaker: SAM
words: the kids did in town then? and "Hide and Go Seek"

speaker: HS
words: Well, I think games of "Run Sheep, Run", "Kick the Can".

speaker: HS
words: All those games were very poptilar. Baseball was a very popular sport with

speaker: HS
words: us. We played it in cow pastures and every place around. It was always

speaker: HS
words: But the social life when I was around from oh, I'd say,sixteen on was

speaker: HS
words: pretty much connected with the churches, the young peoples! . ..

speaker: HS
words: we had our young peoples' society there. A little later on we had a roller

speaker: HS
words: rink which we used. Fourth of Julys were a big event in Moscow and practically

speaker: HS
words: all of it took place on Main Street and the races, horseraces and so on at

speaker: HS
words: the fairground. There was one particular race that they had on Main Street

speaker: HS
words: Fourth of July, I'll always remember was put up standards with a ring

speaker: HS
words: I would judge about two inches in diameter, hung from these standards, and

speaker: HS
words: then weon horseback, had Id make it in a certain time down the street

speaker: HS
words: with a billiard cue and grab these rings. And the one that got the most

speaker: HS
words: rings got the prize. They also had to make it in a certain time. If they

speaker: HS
words: didn't  a certain time, why then they were eliminated. And of

speaker: HS
words: course Main Street was decorated with all kinds of bunting and there were

speaker: HS
words: hot dog stands and various concessions on Main Street. A lot of youngsters'

speaker: HS
words: races. And believe it or not, along about, I thik, 1906 to '10 we had a

speaker: HS
words: minor league baseball team here. And they competed with Pullman, Colfax,

speaker: HS
words: Palouse, the towns around here. And all the players with the exception

speaker: HS
words: of the pitchers and the catcher were unpaid. And some of the people that

speaker: HS
words: played on the team as volunteers were: Crob jf)i% y who played short-stop,

speaker: HS
words: Roy who was connected with Washhgton Water Power, and Hal

speaker: HS
words: whowaa a university atudent. This all took place down on the fairgrounds where

speaker: HS
words: the Rosauer place is now. later become the International Harvester

speaker: HS
words: Company.

speaker: SAM
words: Wat about the early fire at the university? You were a kid when that happened,

speaker: HS
words: Let's see. That happened in 1907, I think or '6. '6, I guess it was. It was

speaker: HS
words: right in there someplace. We were living on a farm at the time and we were notifies

speaker: HS
words: that the university was on fire. Andswe went up to the top of the hill

speaker: HS
words: and saw it from about three miles away. After the building burned down, they

speaker: HS
words: wanted to salvage the brick and they employed a lot of us youngsters to clean

speaker: HS
words: brick. They dynamited the building and there must have been twenty of us up

speaker: HS
words: there cleaning brick. And we were paid by the stack of one hundred, we stacked

speaker: HS
words: 'em up one hundred. Well strike, which I and my

speaker: HS
words: partner didn't strffe. The foreman put us on an easy brick pile where all

speaker: HS
words: we had to do was scrape the brick together and pile them up. That way

speaker: HS
words: we made sixty-five dollars a piece, just cleaning brick at the university.

speaker: HS
words: Well, after that the legislature provided money to start a new

speaker: HS
words: university. Later on, connected with the university after my father passed

speaker: HS
words: away, I worked for George Creighton. I was a messenger and stock keeper.

speaker: HS
words: And in about a year he figured that I ought to learn something about

speaker: HS
words: merchandising so he got somebody else to deliver packages and I worked in

speaker: HS
words: the store as atockboy and eventually started selling merchandise. They were

speaker: HS
words: very good to me, both Mr. Creighton and his wife and it was one of my chores

speaker: HS
words: every Monday Horning to go up to Mrs. Crdghton's house, to get her laundry

speaker: HS
words: and take it down to a lady, Mrs. j who did her laundry for her.

speaker: HS
words: An around about 1910, there were a couple of young men down at David's

speaker: HS
words: Store that quit, had a disagreement with the firm aid they both quit. Well,

speaker: HS
words: one of the fellahs happened to be staying with my mother as a roomer and

speaker: HS
words: a boarder. And he told me about it and he caid,"If you want the job go

speaker: HS
words: down and see Mr. David."So I immediately did ttat, and I went down there

speaker: HS
words: and being Scandinavian, why, and the county at that time was.sixty to seventy

speaker: HS
words: percent Scandinavian anyway, a lot of them didn't talk very good English. So

speaker: HS
words: he employed me at once as a saelsman in the men's department. Well, that

speaker: HS
words: went against the grain of Mr. Creighton, and he thought he was g?Lng to keep

speaker: HS
words: me there for years, I guess. And he didn't speak to me for years afterwards.

speaker: HS
words: And even called up Mr. David and told him, he rays,"What do you mean by taking

speaker: HS
words: my help away from me?" So that was that. I was in the store in the men's

speaker: HS
words: department, and the shoe department and the boy's department up until

speaker: HS
words: World War I. Mr. Homer David was head of the department. And so he wanted

speaker: HS
words: to take over the ready-to wear and the department, and he put me in

speaker: HS
words: as head of the men's department in 1917, I think it was. And I held that

speaker: HS
words: position until the time I retired in 1958.

speaker: SAM
words: Let me ask you this: When you first started in working there for Creightons

speaker: SAM
words: did you have a pretty good idea in your mind that this was the kind of

speaker: SAM
words: work you were going to want to do?

speaker: HS
words: Oh yes, I just loved merchandising. I loved merchandising. And I liked meeting

speaker: HS
words: people. It was easy for me to wait on people for that reason. And in all the

speaker: HS
words: time that I was connected with the store, unless that they were people that

speaker: HS
words: didn't have a charge account, I practically knew em by their first name

speaker: HS
words: because the population at that time of Moscow was around about 3500, and

speaker: HS
words: thejf only had about six hundred enrolled at the university. So we got to know

speaker: HS
words: faculty member and practically every student.

speaker: HS
words: Another thing that the Davids pride themselves on, and I in the history

speaker: HS
words: of their experience in the store, they did a tremendous credit business because

speaker: HS
words: they had a grocery department, furniture department. It was the biggest

speaker: HS
words: department Store in northern Idaho. And they extended credit rather freely

speaker: HS
words: to people. And many is the time they'd give credit. The students, any number

speaker: HS
words: of students would get credit in the fall of the year and they'd operated in

speaker: HS
words: clear until the next fall, wouldn't pay their bill until the next fall. And

speaker: HS
words: they paid it up in the fall by working during the summer. And the farmers

speaker: HS
words: did the same thing, a lot of the farmers paid up once a year. Well, the results

speaker: HS
words: of that credit business, we still found that the students' credit, they

speaker: HS
words: lost  I one percent ot the credit with students.

speaker: HS
words: And the farmers were bigger than that; farmers were a bigger risk than the students

speaker: HS
words: at that time, which is quite a

speaker: HS
words: coincidence. Speaking mbout the store being the biggest store in the

speaker: HS
words: north of Idaho, had a tremendous exchange, bought eggs, bacon and butter

speaker: HS
words: the farmers that brought it in. And for that they got script money

speaker: HS
words: from five cents to five dollars. And some of these people wold collect a

speaker: HS
words: hundred or more dollars in that script to pay up some of the bills that

speaker: HS
words: they had. I've seen many times when they were candling eggs; they would get

speaker: HS
words: in on Friday and Saturday dozens and dozens of eggs. And they'd have to

speaker: HS
words: candle them so there weren't any spoiled eggs. And I've seen lots of eggs

speaker: HS
words: that were spoiled eggs and rotten eggs that people had picked up eggs nests

speaker: HS
words: along fence rows or something and turned em in. And butter, some of it you

speaker: HS
words: could smell almost the time it got into the store. And they took that and

speaker: HS
words: give it a throw into a big , shipped it to Spokane and the bakeries used

speaker: HS
words: it up there in baking. And the surplus of eggs--we got in more eggs

speaker: HS
words: than we could even sell locally, and put em in thirty dozen cases mnd

speaker: HS
words: shipped em up to Spokane a refrigerator up there. And in the season of the

speaker: HS
words: year that we needed those, then we drew on em and b ought em back. So that

speaker: HS
words: was quite a business with bacon and eggs and butter. And the farmers in those

speaker: HS
words: days,.when I was in the store^in 1910 when I went in there, the automobile

speaker: HS
words: still wasn't in use and the farmers only cafe to town where they were off

speaker: HS
words: five or six or ten miles away from town,they would come in two, three times

speaker: HS
words: a year. Most of these farmers had big families—five, six, eight and ten

speaker: HS
words: children and u in a hay rack, hack of some kind and come in

speaker: HS
words: the store Bnd we'd fit every one of the youngsters with underwear, sweaters,

speaker: HS
words: shoes, socks, clothing for the winter. And of course they all attended

speaker: HS
words: country schools so our city schools we just for people in Moscow. So it

speaker: HS
words: was quite a chore,it took you a go8fr °nalf a day to wait on one family,

speaker: HS
words: to fit em out.

speaker: SAM
words: This would be when the farmer did only come in a couple few times a yearj

speaker: HS
words: Yes.

speaker: SAM
words: So this trading trip was a real major experience. Did most of these tend

speaker: SAM
words: to trade locally more than they would in Moscow let's say? Would they save

speaker: SAM
words: Moscow for the big trip?

speaker: HS
words: Moscow was a trading center. There was no other place really for them to

speaker: HS
words: trade because in their own communities like Genesee, Uniontown, Colton

speaker: HS
words: Tain and Kendrick and a those places, they couldn't afford to have the

speaker: HS
words: stocks that the Moscow stores could have. So, Moscow has always been a

speaker: HS
words: trading center for, I would say, thirty miles arountf. Cause today Moscow goes

speaker: HS
words: clear down to up to Tekoa and Oakesdale and all those places, Uniontown

speaker: HS
words: and Colton. It's still the trading center of this area.

speaker: SAM
words: Well, when you first went to work fer David what were the styles that were

speaker: SAM
words: popular and sold?

speaker: HS
words: Well, I remember when I left Creighton's and went down to David's, Creighton's

speaker: HS
words: had clothes, and tHy had quite areputation even at that

speaker: HS
words: time. So I bought a blue serge suit. It's very conservative, three button,

speaker: HS
words: had a vest, and you wore suspenders instead of a belt. And you had a shirt

speaker: HS
words: with a white collar, sometimes a hook-on tie. And then along about 1912 young

speaker: HS
words: men's styles began to get pretty wild. That is, System Clothes of Chicago

speaker: HS
words: came out with a suit; it was peg tops and about a fourteen inch bottom, you

speaker: HS
words: could stick your hands in your pants pockets and pull em out like a lady's

speaker: HS
words: skirt almost, and the front of the coat dipped, and the front of the dip°SJmost

speaker: HS
words: htyour knee in the front. Well, that lasted for about four years. And then we

speaker: HS
words: got into the real English model suit which is the other extreme: vey tight,

speaker: HS
words: short coat, and four button front, and a very tight fitting suit. Well

speaker: HS
words: I was going to the fair in San Francisco in 1915, World's Fair, and I bought one

speaker: HS
words: of those suits and I put it on and it was so extreme that I was afraid

speaker: HS
words: to even wear it around Moscow at the time. So when we went to the fair

speaker: HS
words: I put this suit on and I went down the side street down to the depot, there

speaker: HS
words: were three of us that went to the fair and  Johnson.

speaker: HS
words: of coure And when we got down to San Francisco we Mt at home because we saw all these

speaker: HS
words: types of suits down there.

speaker: SAM
words: So when you went down the side streets, you mean you sort of snuck down to

speaker: SAM
words: the depot?

speaker: HS
words: Yeah, we went down Washington Street. So we had quite a little experience

speaker: HS
words: out of that trip.

speaker: SAM
words: By the why, how did the fair strike you down there? Did you enjoy that

speaker: SAM
words: World's Fair in 1915?

speaker: HS
words: Oh yes,in fact we stayed within fourA blocks of the fairground, and

speaker: HS
words: we spent the whole week out there. It was a big fair. In fact, we enjoyed

speaker: HS
words: it very much. We'd go to town in the evening sometimes. And one of the things

speaker: HS
words: I remember in San Francisco they had free lunch at the beer taverns. And

speaker: HS
words: they served steamed beer, a great big pot of it for five cents. And then you

speaker: HS
words: couli go. to a buffet and help yourself to cheese and crakers and dried fish

speaker: HS
words: and what not. All for the five cent drink of beer.(Chuckles). Then we went

speaker: HS
words: from there down to San Diego and saw the state fair and on down into Tiajuana

speaker: HS
words: and to Mexico to say we'd been out cf the country, we enjoyed that. All of this

speaker: HS
words: trip from here was by train to Portland, and from Portland to San Francisco

speaker: HS
words: by a new boat, the Northern Pacific, that had just been put into service, and

speaker: HS
words: came back by boat.

speaker: SAM
words: Let me ask you about how you got your stock for the store, David's. Where'd

speaker: SAM
words: it come from?

speaker: HS
words: Well, after I had been there for, oh, five or six years, the senior Mr. David

speaker: HS
words: took a liking to me and figured that I was going to be one of them.

speaker: HS
words: So he offered me some stock through the company to be paid for

speaker: HS
words: out of the dividends that the stock made. Well, that stock had doubled

speaker: HS
words: two times in the time that I had it so it was more valuable as

speaker: HS
words: the years went on. And I carried that stock up antil 1957 when the stock

speaker: HS
words: was sold to Mr.Child of Spokane. But I still have toe stock because

speaker: HS
words: Childers didn*t buy the building until, oh, just two years ago and he's aaying

speaker: HS
words: for that over a period of years, and I still get dividends on the stock.

speaker: SAM
words: What about the clothing, where did you come by the selection, how did you

speaker: SAM
words: decide what clothes you wee going to carry?

speaker: HS
words: Well, all through the years the salesmen from the factory would come out

speaker: HS
words: with big trunks full of samples, and some of the models already made up.

speaker: HS
words: And we'd open up the sample them over at the hotel, and wefd spend a half

speaker: HS
words: a day over there picking out merchandise, did that twice a year: fall and

speaker: HS
words: spring. Then in later years, why, oh, I'd say in the fifties sometimes they

speaker: HS
words: made a third trip to -rlit in stocks and so on. But most of the clothing was

speaker: HS
words: bought from salesmen that called on us.

speaker: SAM
words: Would you say that the selection that was available to you was wide?

speaker: HS
words: Yes, we had the same selection as the big city stores.

speaker: HS
words: sometimes, if you were not called on soon enough you lost out on

speaker: HS
words: some of the patterns that they made up into suits. During World War I we

speaker: HS
words: had a lot of the U. S. Army stationed here aft the university, Filled up all

speaker: HS
words: the fraternity houses" dormitories. And when World War I was really in its

speaker: HS
words: heighth we also had a flu epidemic which took thousands of people all over

speaker: HS
words: the United States. I think Moscow lost something like ninety or a hundred

speaker: HS
words: people on the flu. Well, the soldiers coiLdn't come down and shop so I put on

speaker: HS
words: a mask an called on em. I got privilege from the commandant to do that.

speaker: HS
words: And duing that period I sold 675 uniforms to soldiers. I've forgotten

speaker: HS
words: how many--it seems like had about 1800 or 2000 soldiers here. At

speaker: HS
words: the time the soldiers were wearing spiral legging and high cut cord

speaker: HS
words: uniforms and so on and the uniforms 'got were very material,

speaker: HS
words: and they weren't satisfied with it. So the order went out that even a corporal

speaker: HS
words: could buy the same kind of material in a uniform as a colonel. So I went

speaker: HS
words: up there then and took measurements

speaker: HS
words: came in, fitted them. It was quite a boost to business during the8war

speaker: HS
words: years.

speaker: SAM
words: Where did you order the suits from?

speaker: HS
words: Well, some of them from Redback, New Jersey. And some of the better ones from

speaker: HS
words: of Chicago. They were in the uniform business. They were one of

speaker: HS
words: the better clothing concerns in those days.

speaker: SAM
words: Did you see much sickness during the flu epidemic, yourself?

speaker: HS
words: Oh yes, you would just see one fellow one day and he was gone the next, some

speaker: HS
words: of your best friends. So the flu was quite severe. And during the latter

speaker: HS
words: part of the epidemic they found that alcohol, a good shot of bourbon, was

speaker: HS
words: about as good as anything. And of course we had prohibition in those days

speaker: HS
words: and it was hard to get. You could get in a drugstore, they had it.

speaker: HS
words: As far as taking measures and so on was concerned, it wasn't long until

speaker: HS
words: if you made a mistake someplace you corrected it next time. So it wasn't

speaker: HS
words: too difficult. And as far as selling was concerned, why I found out

speaker: HS
words: the bst way to handle people on selling was let them express themselves.

speaker: SS
words: One thing I was curious about was how you learned to fit clothes

speaker: SS
words: and how to sell. How to build up your clientele?

speaker: HS
words: Well,most of these salesmen that called on us, clothing lines,I had

speaker: HS
words: a lot of information from them.'Cause they were old timers.They were

speaker: HS
words: men up in their fifties and sixties that called on us.So they were

speaker: HS
words: well versed in the clothing industry and I got a lot of information

speaker: HS
words: from them. Then we had a tailor,Frank Yangle,who made suits and

speaker: HS
words: also did our altering for us.I observed his knowledge of tailoring.

speaker: HS
words: Got to know how the inside of a suit was made. Could see him making

speaker: HS
words: them. not to be high powered in selling. And I think that was a mistake with a

speaker: HS
words: lot of salesmen. The customers didn't have very much confidence in em.

speaker: HS
words: And then after you have satisfied a customer and he's gotten what you told

speaker: HS
words: him it was, he came back, and he wanted you to wait on him agin. I found

speaker: HS
words: that very true after I retired that they called me in I twice a year

speaker: HS
words: for about twelve years on their sales. And of course I dozens and

speaker: HS
words: dozens of people that I've fitt ed suits in years before would see me and they'd

speaker: HS
words: say,"I want to see you." So I thiik it was building up confidence in yourself

speaker: HS
words: in the customer that. And David's was a store that made good on faulty

speaker: HS
words: merchandise. If they got something that didn't hold up to what it should they

speaker: HS
words: made it good. And that was the success. And when they turned the store over

speaker: HS
words: to Mr. Childers they turned over a lot of goodwill that helped him establish

speaker: HS
words: himself.

speaker: SAM
words: Did you say that selling to women was more important than.man on man's clothes

speaker: SAM
words: usually?

speaker: HS
words: Oh, I thitkworaen have had influence on men's wearing apparel for years and years.

speaker: HS
words: I thitk they have a little better idea of matching a tie to a shirt or a

speaker: HS
words: tie to a suit. And I thitk you have to appeal to the women in selling a man.

speaker: HS
words: And once in a while you get a man that says, "No, I'm going to have what I

speaker: HS
words: want."Butif you appeal to the woman your job is half done on selling merchandise.

speaker: HS
words: Women are pretty smart shoppers and some of em are pretty tough customers to

speaker: HS
words: wait on. So you can't contradict them or you're through. You got to let them

speaker: HS
words: know that they know their business. And I think that's success in sellings

speaker: SAM
words: Did you have many customers that just were hard boiled and knew just what

speaker: SAM
words: they wanted and wouldn't take anybody's word for it except theirs?

speaker: HS
words: Well, I'll take this one woman because she's dead and gone and it's

speaker: HS
words: nothing against her. She was born in Sweden and knew woolen materials,

speaker: HS
words: knew linen from early childhood and she had a big family of eight. They were

speaker: HS
words: farmers and she'd come in the store to—her husband couldn't even buy a

speaker: HS
words: suit without her okay. And she would examine the material. If I had told her

speaker: HS
words: it was all wool, she^ould say,"Well, let's see if it's all wool."And she

speaker: HS
words: would get to the bottom of the trousers where she cold get ahold of a piece

speaker: HS
words: of yarn and she would pull it out and see whether it was wool or not and

speaker: HS
words: how long a wool it was. And then she even burnt to smeel the wool. And when

speaker: HS
words: those tests were made she was satisfied. Well, she did fuying for

speaker: HS
words: youngsters until they were young men and women and left home. But she was a

speaker: HS
words: very shrewd buyer. I remember one winter there was a woolen mills

speaker: HS
words: come out here with winter underwear. Everybody wore bng underwear in those

speaker: HS
words: days practically. And we sold her, because it was good, long wool, wool

speaker: HS
words: underwear for the family. I don't know when we got away from two piece

speaker: HS
words: underwear; I've kind of forgotten when we got into unionsuits. And now we're

speaker: HS
words: back to two piece again, have been for a good many years sitce the jockey

speaker: HS
words: short came out.

speaker: SAM
words: Just one more questio n about women shoppers. Were men usually accompanied

speaker: SAM
words: by their wives when they came into the store to buy clothing?

speaker: HS
words: The final purchase was, but plenty of times the wife came in before to find

speaker: HS
words: out what there was that she thought her husband out to have.

speaker: SAM
words: So you usually were selling the clothes to a couple more than to just a man.

speaker: HS
words: Selling a suit, yes. That was in the earlier days. Today it's different

speaker: HS
words: because you find a lot of young men that buy their own suits, they're not

speaker: HS
words: married a lot of coursse, and some of them are professional  just

speaker: HS
words: go in and buy a suit and that's it.

speaker: SAM
words: What did suits cost when you first started working?

speaker: HS
words: Well, I remember you could get a good, all wool, ounce, blue

speaker: HS
words: serge suit for around seventeen to twenty dollars. Now that wasn't the

speaker: HS
words: best of tailoring. But you cold get the best of tailoring in what was

speaker: HS
words: considered the best make suits in those days around twenty-five to thirty-five

speaker: HS
words: dollars. That was about tops. I have a book down in the basement that I

speaker: HS
words: kept track of all the suits that we sold from 1910 until 1950, I guess.

speaker: HS
words: It'8 a great one to look over because I can go back and see some of the

speaker: HS
words: old, oldtime customers. And I have gone thrQugh that. Ishould have had that

speaker: HS
words: book up here. But the average sale of suits, and we would sell around five,

speaker: HS
words: six hundred suits a year. The average wold run around—one year it seems to

speaker: HS
words: me like it was around eighteen dollars and something and then it went up to

speaker: HS
words: twenty some odd. But we sold a lot of suits for twelve, fifteen dollars.

speaker: SAM
words: Did people have as many occasions to dress formally in the early days as

speaker: SAM
words: they do Would they have rany suits or

speaker: HS
words: Yes. I remember even the fraternity houses, this'll go back before World

speaker: HS
words: War I, used to buy full dress suits, long taled suits, wing collars and

speaker: HS
words: all that. And then we had a few social' groups in town here that used to

speaker: HS
words: have monthly parties. And they would dress up in full dress suits. And

speaker: HS
words: that carried on up until, I would say, about the Depression, '31 or 32.

speaker: HS
words: And then they switched right over to a tuxedo. And as far a full dress was

speaker: HS
words: concerned, except for professional performers, for dances and social affairs,

speaker: HS
words: it's been tuxedos ever since.

speaker: SAM
words: Did you sell jackets in those dayd or was it all suits?

speaker: HS
words: All suits, there were no sport coats. I don't think the sport coat got into

speaker: HS
words: the field until after World War II, it seems to me.

speaker: SAM
words: Okay, how about casual clothes and work clotes? What kind of clothes

speaker: SAM
words: did you sell for those purposes?

speaker: HS
words: Well we didn't have the khaki slacks like you see so much of today, and

speaker: HS
words: it was mostly overalls: striped overalls, blue overalls. And of course, of

speaker: HS
words: the makes,Levi Strauss, Levis were the predominant overall, waist overall.

speaker: HS
words: And was another make that was good, there was one made back in Milwaukee, I've forgotten

speaker: HS
words: the name of that one—

speaker: HS
words: Oshkosh, that was a very popular one. And work shirts—I'm amazed at the

speaker: HS
words: price of  work shirts that I see on the counter today. I sold

speaker: HS
words: hundreds and hundreds of dozens of Chanukry shirts at thirty-nine cents a

speaker: HS
words: piece. And today a Chambry shirt runs six dollars. So it was quite a

speaker: HS
words: difference there. And of course, men's socks, you had the old Rockford

speaker: HS
words: sock for a work sock and you wore garters, suspenders, except on Levis.

speaker: SAM
words: What's the Rockford sock made out of?

speaker: HS
words: Cotton. And of course wool socks in the wintertime. And these Rockford socks

speaker: HS
words: only sold for five to fifteen cents a piece, you know, in those days.

speaker: HS
words: Canvas gloves were five to twenty-five cents. a good pair of shoes for

speaker: HS
words: five dollars.

speaker: SAM
words: Did you sell a lot of work boots in those days?

speaker: HS
words: Yes, we had a logging business all around this area and we sold a lot of

speaker: HS
words: caulked boots because they needed those in the timber because there was

speaker: HS
words: a lot of what you call gyppoing in the lumber mills. And all those log

speaker: HS
words: had to be drug down by the ponds and the mills and so on. Yeah, we sold

speaker: HS
words: women's

speaker: HS
words: a lot of boots. And in the early days* men's shoes were all six inch tops,

speaker: HS
words: they were Oxfords in those days. And in women's shoes there was a period

speaker: HS
words: there, I think before World War Y,e?adies wore laced dress shoes, you'd

speaker: HS
words: call em boots today, they came clear up below the^ knew and lace em up.

speaker: SAM
words: What about casual clothing? Was there anything in between the work clothes

speaker: SAM
words: and the dress clothes for just an everyday, like we are today? Everyday wear?

speaker: HS
words: Well, we wore sweaters inside of a coat some of the time. And oh, we did

speaker: HS
words: have some odd trousers made out of wool,;ho cotton trousers that I remember

speaker: HS
words: of, andnsport ahirts or anything like that. In fact I don't remember a

speaker: HS
words: shirt with a collar on it until up in the twenties. Before that time you

speaker: HS
words: wore a white collar.

speaker: SAM
words: Well, how did the students'? styles differ from what you  to the townspeople?

speaker: HS
words: Well, like today they wore more extreme. Like the LSystem suit I was

speaker: HS
words: telling you about. Not many of the townpeople bought that type of a suit, but

speaker: HS
words: the students did. And as years have gone by the students have been important

speaker: HS
words: in styles of men's clothing. Today the younger people set more styles than

speaker: HS
words: the older people do. That's Were sportswear came in, and they were the ones

speaker: HS
words: the originated that.

speaker: SAM
words: Do you thiik that the university was as important to business in the early

speaker: SAM
words: days as it has become in recent years ttere?

speaker: HS
words: Well, when you figure the difference in population, we only had a population

speaker: HS
words: in those early days of oh, probably not over four thousand, less than that

speaker: HS
words: probably. Ahad a student body of around six hundred to nine hundred. That

speaker: HS
words: would compare very well with our population today of fifteen thousand and

speaker: HS
words: seven thousand students. So I would say that the students business has always

speaker: HS
words: been a factor in merchandising here. Farming of course is the biggest business

speaker: HS
words: we have. And today the university business is a big factor, and it is in

speaker: HS
words: Moscow because we have two universities so close together because a tremendous

speaker: HS
words: amount of Pullman people trade in Moscow, not only students but faculty.

speaker: SAM
words: What about your competition in the early days? Did you have much in clothing

speaker: SAM
words: from other stores?

speaker: HS
words: Yes, we did. We had a merchant come into Moscow named

speaker: HS
words: who opened up a store down where J. Cenney is now, much smaller quarters

speaker: HS
words: than J. C. Penney. And then later moved up to where theHha tuna Apartments

speaker: HS
words: are today. And he had the whole three floors of mercandise. And he was then

speaker: HS
words: the biggest merchandiser in northern Idaho, overtook David's. And even had a

speaker: HS
words: restaurant connected with it so people could lunch there, had an elevator and

speaker: HS
words: all that. And he was a promoter, he%d sales and he was an Irishman and he

speaker: HS
words: had a lot on the ball. And he was competitive with us, but it may have hurt

speaker: HS
words: us some but it also helped us in the long runrhaving a store of that type

speaker: HS
words: brought more people in from the outside. We had more trading area people.

speaker: HS
words: He was in business there up until, it seems to me, just before World War II.

speaker: HS
words: But he and another fellow got interested in the stock business, full-blooded

speaker: HS
words: stock business and he got putting all his money into that and it affected

speaker: HS
words: the store; he finally went out of business. His son took part of the stock

speaker: HS
words: and opened up over at Palouse, Frank Williamson. He was a promoter. In

speaker: HS
words: fact,he was instigator of one of the first fairs in Moscow,and that's back

speaker: HS
words: before 1910. He was quite a man.

speaker: SAM
words: Would you say in general there was a lot of competition in Moscow among the

speaker: SAM
words: becinesses?

speaker: HS
words: Well, Creighton's were competitive. They had their clientele, they had a

speaker: HS
words: certain brand of merchandise that people liked and they went there for it.

speaker: HS
words: We had another store, Oscar that opened up but he didn't last very

speaker: HS
words: long. We had the fashion shop which was Fanny Stewart, early days. He sold

speaker: HS
words: out and it was t competition. We had Carter and Hanes, a men's shop,

speaker: HS
words: a specialty shop that opened up, but they didn't last too long. The store

speaker: HS
words: weathered a lot of those people. And then there was another men's shop opened

speaker: HS
words: Rhodes, out of Lewiston opened up. And they didn't last very long. I

speaker: HS
words: went down and bought their stock when they closed out twenty-five cents

speaker: HS
words: on the dollar and sold it out on sale. So while we've had lots of comperfon

speaker: HS
words: over the years we withstood it all and a lot of em didn't weather it. And

speaker: HS
words: the reason why the store decided to sell out is that they were getting

speaker: HS
words: too old and they felt that while the store still had a good name, goodwill,

speaker: HS
words: that they'd dispose of it.

speaker: HS
words: Well, there were two or three chain stores that wanted to buy it. But

speaker: HS
words: for the reputation of the store, they would never sell it to a chain. And

speaker: HS
words: that's how Mr. Childers happened to get it out of Spokane. He's an independent.

speaker: HS
words: And he's operated it now for, well sixteen, seventeen years, and he's

speaker: HS
words: very well.

speaker: SAM
words: How did the stock that was carried at David's, how was that different than

speaker: SAM
words: the stock that was carried at Creighton's in the men's line, what was the

speaker: SAM
words: difference between the two?

speaker: HS
words: Not much different except David's had a much bigger stock. We had a bigger

speaker: HS
words: clothing stock, we had a bigger shirt s^Bck. I don't think Creighton's had

speaker: HS
words: work clothing at all, but they had a shoe department and they had a rug

speaker: HS
words: department. So where a customer wanted a assortment they went

speaker: HS
words: to David's

speaker: SAM
words: Was there something like the Chamber of Commerce now is among the businessmen

speaker: SAM
words: in Moscow in the old days?

speaker: HS
words: The Chamber of Commerce?

speaker: SAM
words: I'm wondering how the businesses got along, competing with each other

speaker: SAM
words: and co-operating with each other both in the early days, not just clothing

speaker: SAM
words: but in general on Main Street.

speaker: HS
words: I think it was pretty fair. I don't think there was much--oh, there was a time

speaker: HS
words: when the drugstores, when Frank Robinson came in here and opened up his

speaker: HS
words: and he opened up a drugstore on ths corner of Sixth and Main, and

speaker: HS
words: he opened up another one where Myklebust's are And of course he disposed

speaker: HS
words: both of em later but the other drugstores were very put out about his system

speaker: HS
words: of doing business. But it's no different from today, pharmacists have a lot

speaker: HS
words: of competition today. You've got your Drug Fair that came in here now that

speaker: HS
words: are appealing to pharmaceutical needs. But I thitk things in general up and

speaker: HS
words: down Main Street was pretty good because they. The Chamber of Commerce, I

speaker: HS
words: don't know when the Chamber of Commerce was organized, but it was pretty early.

speaker: HS
words: And they co-operated very well, donations and sponsoring the Fourth of July

speaker: HS
words: and the fair and different things that came to Moscow:circuses, even had a

speaker: HS
words: chataqua here three different years that the city sponsored. You know, when

speaker: HS
words: we came here in fc2, there were a lot of false front buildings. By that

speaker: HS
words: I mean there was a false upper part to cover up the back part so it made

speaker: HS
words: that store look like it was bigger than it was. But there are still standing,

speaker: HS
words: permanent buildings that were here.when we came here and that8the Moscow Hotel,

speaker: HS
words: the telephone building, which is the old the Owl Drugstore,

speaker: HS
words: which was the Hardware Store, and up above harware store was a business

speaker: HS
words: school run by T. M. he ran that for a good many years.

speaker: HS
words: Another

speaker: HS
words: fellow named tarried it on. David's store building was built by

speaker: HS
words: Kaufman back in the late '80s, must have been '87 or '8 or around in there.

speaker: HS
words: And the McConnell- Building was built back in the *80's, Creighton's

speaker: HS
words: Store, that's an old building. But there were a lot of wooden buildings on

speaker: HS
words: Main Street, an awful lot of wooden buildings. And of course the streets

speaker: HS
words: weren't paved on Main Street until about 1910. Before that time it was mud,

speaker: HS
words: and then they covered the mud with rock. It was a pretty messy set-up. And

speaker: HS
words: we didn't have cement sidewalks, we had board sidewalks, just like you

speaker: HS
words: found in the pioneer towns. The town was really divided into four sections

speaker: HS
words: in the early days. The center of town actually was Main and Sixth. From

speaker: HS
words: Sixth Street and Mair noith and west was the Lieuallen addition. And west

speaker: HS
words: and south was the Deakin division, and then up in Swede town and all that

speaker: HS
words: was the McGregor addition, and the other was the Russell addition. Howe

speaker: HS
words: happened this Third and Main being the center I don't know but that

speaker: HS
words: happened to be where the property lines wire aseyI understand it donated

speaker: HS
words: the main street off of their property. And Russell of course donated the

speaker: HS
words: of ground wherethe Russell School is. And M Gregor owned the boarding

speaker: HS
words: house where the Gritman Hospital is which he ran a boarding house and hotel

speaker: HS
words: there for some years, and then it was converted into a hospital by Mr. Gritman.

speaker: HS
words: And he operated that until the present structure was built. Think of having

speaker: HS
words: a blacksmith shop right where the Kenworthy theatre is. A blacksmith shop

speaker: HS
words: and a carriage snp, were operated by Zumhoff who was a character in the

speaker: HS
words: early days here in Moscow and Major Collins who did most of the horseshoeing

speaker: HS
words: and sfe on. And then Chris Anderson used to make wheels, repair carriages

speaker: HS
words: and all that. That was right on Main Steet.

speaker: SAM
words: What do you remember about Zumhoff? Why he was such a character?

speaker: HS
words: Well, he was a boisterous type of a fellow. He was short tempered but tender

speaker: HS
words: hearted atjfat. He was, I thidc, one of first firemen of our fire departmnt

speaker: HS
words: which was a volunteer fire department. We had three or four of em that were

speaker: HS
words: in different sections of the town. We didn't have fire engines, they just had a.

speaker: HS
words: with a hose until later on. But Zumhoff was, as I say, as honest as the

speaker: HS
words: day is kng, but he was short tempered. He told you exactly what he thought

speaker: HS
words: whether you liked it or not, had a wonderful family of four girls, all went into

speaker: HS
words: the teaching field. I had one of them that was a Latin teacher in high school.

speaker: HS
words: And one of my first jobs that I had after my father passed away was

speaker: HS
words: piling six cords of sawed wood for M. F. Zumhoff into his woodshed, which

speaker: HS
words: he lived right back of the blacksmith shop. That was one of the first jobs

speaker: HS
words: I had.

speaker: SAM
words: What kind of tradition did they have about the old stores that had been

speaker: SAM
words: there before you were there? What do you think they thought about Durham

speaker: SAM
words: and Kaufmn, when people talked about them in the pfneer times?

speaker: HS
words: Well, Durham and Kaufman came out of the midwest,and they were Jewish,

speaker: HS
words: both of them. And they knew the business angle from A to Z. And they had a

speaker: HS
words: story—and I've forgotten whether it was Kendrick or Juliaetta also. And they

speaker: HS
words: were, of course, shrewd buyers, and they knew how to make a profit on

speaker: HS
words: merchandise. Well, they were here during the wet year when they didn't get

speaker: HS
words: to harvest their wheat.And of course, they did a credit business with the

speaker: HS
words: farmers, just like David's did later on. And some of those bills would run

speaker: HS
words: up twelve, fifteen hundred dollars and they couldn't pay 'em. So he would

speaker: HS
words: take a mortgage on their place or a mortgage. And from what I

speaker: HS
words: understand they had a good many thousands'tied up in those mortgages. Well,

speaker: HS
words: it got to the point where they had to have money too, so they foreclosed on

speaker: HS
words: em. And it left a bitter taste in the whole community here over the two.

speaker: HS
words: And it eventually practically drove them out of Moscow because of that. So

speaker: HS
words: they left here in the nineties sometime and went down to San Francisco and

speaker: HS
words: opened up the Emporium which finally become the biggest retail store on the

speaker: HS
words: Pacific coast. I've been in the store even when they hadn't moved in fliere,

speaker: HS
words: of course. And today of course itfs dwindled down now till what it isn't what

speaker: HS
words: it used to be. And the orginal Durham and Kaufman, even their families are

speaker: HS
words: out of it too. But that, I would judge they were here about ten years; enough

speaker: HS
words: that they accumulated quite a bit of money.

speaker: SAM
words: I've heard there was bad feeling towards Vollmer in the Troy area because

speaker: SAM
words: of foreclosure during that time.

speaker: HS
words: I've understood that too, especially arod the Genesee area. And in fact,

speaker: HS
words: the town of Troy used to be called Vollmer, you know. And I think that that

speaker: HS
words: experience is why they changed it to Troy. But Vollmer did foreclose on,

speaker: HS
words: and that's where he got a lot of his land. But there were a lot of things

speaker: HS
words: that Vollmer did for this whole area then we were still Nez Perce County*

speaker: HS
words: He was in the banking business as well as the real estate business. And if

speaker: HS
words: I'm not mistaken he was a promoter of a railroad from Missoula down the

speaker: HS
words: Lewis and Clark Highway in to Lewiston which didn't pass but he tried to

speaker: HS
words: promote that. And eventually came through this way and down to Lewiston, and

speaker: HS
words: then from Lewiston out to iAAlond* or whatever that town if in the other

speaker: HS
words: direction.

speaker: SAM
words: What about McCodell's store, did they talk about that when you were a

speaker: SAM
words: young man, about what kind of an operation McConnell had?

speaker: HS
words: Well, it was a complete department store, groceries and all. And they used

speaker: HS
words: three floors of building too. And it probably would have been in business

speaker: HS
words: today had Governor McConnell not accepted the governorship of Idaho. He went

speaker: HS
words: down and spent four years as governor down there, and for some reason, it

speaker: HS
words: may have been the times. Well, ofcourse that was during that wet year too;

speaker: HS
words: that what affected him too 'cause he was governor from 1893 to '96—four

speaker: HS
words: years. And by the way, Mr. F. A. David was head of the grocery department in

speaker: HS
words: the McConnell-McGuire store. He had a stock; he had money in it, and he

speaker: HS
words: lost it all because McConnell-McGuire Store did not go bankrupt like some

speaker: HS
words: people thought. He just simply closed it up and sold his merchandise and

speaker: HS
words: mortgaged his own home and all that. He paid his bills. Maybe he didn't pay

speaker: HS
words: a hundred percent on the dollar, but he didn't go into bankruptcy. And after

speaker: HS
words: that experience McConnell of ccttrse was elected—I've forgotten, Indian agency

speaker: HS
words: or something. And then later on he was immigration officer for years here.

speaker: HS
words: Hsd his offices in the old post office building there. No, they had a wonderful

speaker: HS
words: store Up there and did a good business.

speaker: SAM
words: So what about that? You were telling me about the kidding back and

speaker: SAM
words: forth that went on between the groceries and the clothing in David's.

speaker: HS
words: Well, the groceries department was at the rear of the store. And they

speaker: HS
words: carried high grade chocolates which all the help liked. And eve*y once in

speaker: HS
words: a while why, when they weren't looking, why we'd go back there and help our

speaker: HS
words: selves to a chocolate. And then they even had a reserve stock of candy in

speaker: HS
words: a room that the elevator passed, and all you had to do was step off the

speaker: HS
words: elevator and go in there and help yourself there. Well it got to be so bad

speaker: HS
words: that the firm put a padlock on that door and instructed grocerman to tell

speaker: HS
words: every to keep out of it. But that didn't entirely solve the problem.

speaker: HS
words: So one of the groceryasn decided to put some paregoric in some of the

speaker: HS
words: chocolates on the top. And they knew which ones they were. And so one of

speaker: HS
words: the girls in the dry goods department went back ttfre and she get one of those.

speaker: HS
words: And it sure made her sick. She had to go home and her parents even came down

speaker: HS
words: to store and took it up in the store that somebody had pulled something on.

speaker: HS
words: But they came out of it all right. And then they used to buy watermelons,

speaker: HS
words: bought a hay rack load and line 'em up on the floor. Well, there was kind of

speaker: HS
words: an opening between th£. dry goods department and the men's department and the

speaker: HS
words: grocery depaitment, and of course thy couldn't sell a cracked watermelon,so

speaker: HS
words: every once in a while one of the fellows, during watermelon season would go

speaker: HS
words: down there and kick one of those watermelons to crack it.That way they had

speaker: HS
words: a watermelon feed. And Mr. David, coming ordinally from Wisconsin, was a great

speaker: HS
words: cheese man. And he used to buy cheese by the carload. And he would cure it;

speaker: HS
words: he'd get it fresh and then he'd cure it on the top  of the storey and

speaker: HS
words: then bring It down. They were great big--oh, they would weigh five hundred

speaker: HS
words: pounds, I guess, some of those cheese. And they'd put it in a cheese cutter

speaker: HS
words: and you'd come and you'd buy any amount of cheese you want.

speaker: HS
words: Sugar tie same way. I remember sugar in those days ysu'd

speaker: HS
words: get a hundred pounds for $4.95. Today you're payin forty dollars for a

speaker: HS
words: hundred pounds.

speaker: SAM
words: He used to cure cheese on the roof?

speaker: HS
words: No, this was in the third floor; no, it wasn't on the roof. It was hot

speaker: HS
words: of us in the store would go up there and

speaker: HS
words: turn 'em every so often so that they wouldn't get cured one-sided. And that

speaker: HS
words: was quite a job. And then in late August they'd have the fraternity and

speaker: HS
words: sorrity houses and dormitories come down and the canned goods people would

speaker: HS
words: have a display of their canned goodo up on the third fldor and have samples and

speaker: HS
words: they'd go up there and sample it. Someof it was water, some of it was sugar

speaker: HS
words: and they'd buy their supply of canned goods for the winter that way. That's

speaker: HS
words: one reason why the grocery store at David's was a very big part of business

speaker: HS
words: in those days.

speaker: SAM
words: Did students have any trouble getting credit from David's?

speaker: HS
words: No, No, all they had to do was to put a reference down, and that mostly was

speaker: HS
words: their folks. And in later years we wouldvrite their folks and tell them that

speaker: HS
words: your son or daughter has come in and applied for credit and we've accepted him,

speaker: HS
words: and we want your sanction as to whether he should have credit or for how much.

speaker: HS
words: And they got some of the finest letters back from parents thanking them for

speaker: HS
words: informing em about it and in some cases they let em have free rein and in

speaker: HS
words: some cases they put a limit on em and so on. So it out very, very fine.

speaker: SAM
words: I'd like to change the subject and talk some about I recreation and social

speaker: SAM
words: life. And before talking about it in later years, one event of your childhood

speaker: SAM
words: that I noticed here that you mentioned before was the flood they had between

speaker: SAM
words: Moscow and Pullman. When you were a kid you saw that, right?

speaker: HS
words: That's right. Well, that was in around 1918. And I've forgotten what time of

speaker: HS
words: the year. It seems like it was in January or February we had a great thaw and

speaker: HS
words: then right after the thaw it froze stiff as a board. Everybody got out their

speaker: HS
words: skates and we were to skate on the Pullman Highway. where it

speaker: HS
words: is now. But it was all flooded clear across there and we could skate down to

speaker: HS
words: Pullman. And that lasted for about two weeks. Everybody was out on skates.

speaker: HS
words: I know I got a  German skates that I had for years. So that

speaker: HS
words: was I think about the worst flood we've ever had. Oh, we may have had one more

speaker: HS
words: that Pullman got pretty hard where there was about three feet of water in the

speaker: HS
words: Main Street of Pullman. And sandbags didn't even hold it. But in later years

speaker: HS
words: they haven't had any trouble to speak of.

speaker: SAM
words: Well, what was the social life like in the twenties in the community in those

speaker: SAM
words: early years before World War I?

speaker: HS
words: Well, we had a Moscow band for one thing. And I happened to be in the band, played

speaker: HS
words: cornet. And we had a hayrack fixed up with seats. And every Friday night we'd

speaker: HS
words: play on Main Street, a different corner every Friday night so that each business

speaker: HS
words: house would have some publicity out of it. Well, we did that for a good many years

speaker: HS
words: and then some Sundays we gave a concert in the park. Well, in 1912, our band

speaker: HS
words: was a pretty good band, Ed Cleary waa the leader. And the Rose Festival over in

speaker: HS
words: Portland, the Elks wanted to send the band over there and it developed into a

speaker: HS
words: north Idaho band. So we had forty-eight musicians and we went over to the

speaker: HS
words: Portland Rose Show and we competed against forty-eight bands and came home

speaker: HS
words: with second prize on that. Well, that was an incentive to keep the band going.

speaker: SAM
words: What kind of music did you play?

speaker: HS
words: Well, it was a ragtime music; "Oh you Beautiful Doll" and "Alexander's Ragtime

speaker: HS
words: Band," and there was a new piece that came out at that time and when, i

speaker: HS
words: stop, we'd play on corners and people would get out in the street and

speaker: HS
words: dance. There was a band of music that people liked, had lots of harmony in it,

speaker: HS
words: and they enjoyed it. I know evenings we used to go around to the different

speaker: HS
words: rathskellers and play and people would be dancing on top of the tables even.

speaker: HS
words: It was that interesting.

speaker: SAM
words: How many were there in the band?

speaker: HS
words: Oh I think we had about think it was forty-eight pieces in the band,

speaker: HS
words: though our city band wasn't that big, no.

speaker: SAM
words: That's what I meant, the city band.

speaker: HS
words: No, our city band was twenty some odd pieces. But we enjoyed it. And then

speaker: HS
words: that of course we formed a dance orchestra which I played in at the old

speaker: HS
words: ten cents a dance. And there were evenings that we would make

speaker: HS
words: as much as ten, twelve, fifteen dollars and night a piece, playing for that.

speaker: SAM
words: Did you keep the numbers pretty short?

speaker: HS
words: Yeah, you didn't give any encores, I'll tell you. And then our Moscow band did

speaker: HS
words: lots of playing around other territory here. We went.to Potlatch and played for

speaker: HS
words: their celebrations. They used to have a lot of celebrations there during the

speaker: HS
words: real lumber business up there. Andover to Colfax and down to froy and around.

speaker: SAM
words: I've been told that some religious groups had a pretty strong gripe about

speaker: SAM
words: dancing at the time, didn't feel that it was good for people in Moscow.

speaker: HS
words: Well, there was a fellow from Spokane came down here and started a mandolin

speaker: HS
words: and guitar club. And it seems to me he gave em all lessons in one class, he

speaker: HS
words: take there didn't you individually. And I've forgotten, " must have been 25 or 30

speaker: HS
words: of us that took those lessons and after we had ctmpleted the course, I think he

speaker: HS
words: gave us eight or ten lessons, he gave a concert in the Methodist Church. Well,

speaker: HS
words: that disbanded the organization, but there were my sister and myself and

speaker: HS
words: Eggan and Jenny Peterson formed a little group of our own. And we

speaker: HS
words: played at lodges and churches and young people's places, the pieces that we

speaker: HS
words: played. Well, we were invited to play at the Lutheran church one Sunday, which

speaker: HS
words: we did. And of course we played waltzes and two-steps. Well, there

speaker: HS
words: conservative Lutherans in the group that thought it was out of place. And

speaker: HS
words: we got a little criticism on that so we never played in a church after that.

speaker: SAM
words: I guess what I heard recently was that there was some feeling that if they

speaker: SAM
words: didn't like drink they feltthat any drinking was wrong, might also feel

speaker: SAM
words: that dancing was wrong, perhaps. That some people felt that way—that they

speaker: SAM
words: were both wrong.

speaker: HS
words: Well, the Methodist church was pretty strict on dancing and playing cards

speaker: HS
words: in the early days here. That was taboj? And I thine it wasn't till up in the

speaker: HS
words: 20's, I guess before dancing generally got to be more popular. I know when

speaker: HS
words: the Mormons came in here in the 20's, they're great on youth work and they

speaker: HS
words: allow dancing in their recreational room and things of that type. And that

speaker: HS
words: helped things out a little. So there was a group that was against both dancing

speaker: HS
words: and cards. But we've got a different civilization today.

speaker: SAM
words: What about your work with the scouts? When did that start in?

speaker: HS
words: Well, that started around a Sunday schoolclass in the Presbyterian church

speaker: HS
words: in 1920. There was a lady teacher who had seven boys. And they were all boys

speaker: HS
words: that had a lot on the ball. Hie was at a loss to know what to entertain them

speaker: HS
words: with. So she nsked me if I would help her out, which I did. And I formed a little

speaker: HS
words: athletic club around her class,) which we met every week, and then we took a few

speaker: HS
words: hikes and so on. A little later on there was a scout troop in town hebecame

speaker: HS
words: an excutive, and he was interested in organizing the town in scouting. So he

speaker: HS
words: came to me and asked me if I wouldn't like to convert that class into a scout

speaker: HS
words: and then enlarge it. So I go along with him at first because we

speaker: HS
words: were having a good program as it was. But I eventually fell for it, and I knew

speaker: HS
words: nothing about scouting. So when we went into it we started out with these

speaker: HS
words: seven boys and we built it up to thirty-two boys of the community. They weren't

speaker: HS
words: Presbyterians by any means. And we had a very interesting program. We had

speaker: HS
words: good backing from the church and from the parents and through that we developed

speaker: HS
words: a campsite out on Moscow Mountain which we used quite often. And every year

speaker: HS
words: between Christmas and New Year's we'd go out for a three day stay in the

speaker: HS
words: mountains in the wintertime and take our skiis and toboggans out there.A

speaker: HS
words: one year I was just overloaded with boys, I took boys in from other troops.

speaker: HS
words: We had forty-elnt boys out there. We went out there in dry weather, went out in

speaker: HS
words: cars, some boys hiked out, and when we were up there it snowed. And the parents

speaker: HS
words: got a little concerned about us being up there so they got ahold of a couple

speaker: HS
words: of bobsleds and went out there to bring us back to town. Well, the camp was over

speaker: HS
words: and we wens just getting ready to hike back in when they picked us up. Well, the

speaker: HS
words: nice thing about that particular campsite was that we had a cabin up there, a

speaker: HS
words: house up there that a banker had built years ago as a kind of simmer resort and

speaker: HS
words: he abandoned it and we fixed it up- put bunks in there and got bedsprings from

speaker: HS
words: town. And we housed all the boys in that house except twelve. And they had to

speaker: HS
words: build lean-tos outside which they'd been taught how to build and they slept out

speaker: HS
words: in the open which they got a ilk out of they loved it. And feeding em was

speaker: HS
words: quite a problem because some boys liked sotiic things and some didn't. And I

speaker: HS
words: had my older scouts act as cooks with supervision. And we had good meals and

speaker: HS
words: they enjoyed em and we wound up to the last day wen we wanted to get rid of every

speaker: HS
words: thing. So we had a great big kettle.we made stew in so we took bread, we took

speaker: HS
words: eggs that we had left, we had peas and potatoes, I've forgotten—anyway we

speaker: HS
words: dumped the whole works into the pot and let it boil. And there wasn't a boy

speaker: HS
words: in the camp that didn't think^it was great. (Chuckles). Some of the boys that

speaker: HS
words: were picky wouldn't eat this or that had to eat it or starve. They

speaker: HS
words: found out that they liked it.

speaker: SAM
words: What kinds of skills did you teach the boys in scouting then?

speaker: HS
words: Well, it was the regular scout program of trustworthiness and ., helpful,

speaker: HS
words: friendly, kind, obedient, cheerful, brava, clean, and reverent. Those were all

speaker: HS
words: taught them with the different tests that they took like cooking, fire building,

speaker: HS
words: hiking, swimming, schoolwork, religious wrk all combined into the program

speaker: HS
words: that made a character building organization of it. I had three scout troops

speaker: HS
words: in my time and was troop committeeman the rest of the time 'cause I spent about

speaker: HS
words: years in scouting. And when I completed I got the highest award that scouting

speaker: HS
words: gives you, the silver  which was awared me later.

speaker: SAM
words: So it was a lot more than camping skills, but were camping skills very much

speaker: SAM
words: a

speaker: SAM
words: part of it, to get along, survive in the outdoors?

speaker: HS
words: Well, hiking and camping because nature is one of the programs that's in scouting,

speaker: HS
words: and there is one test that every boy has to a fourteen mile hike and

speaker: HS
words: make observations on his hike: what he saw, perform a good turn on that hike,

speaker: HS
words: and return the same way, and then write up a two hundred word theme onwhat he

speaker: HS
words: saw and did. I have dozens of those filed away now that when some of these older

speaker: HS
words: scouts come here and visit with me I get those out and I read em to em to see

speaker: HS
words: 'they remember what they did, on that fourteen mile hike. Now I think it's a

speaker: HS
words: wonderful program and of course all boys don't enter into it full-heartedly, but

speaker: HS
words: most of em do. And for that reason in 1926 the Kiwanis Club, I was a

speaker: HS
words: member of Kiwanis, scouting had dropped tremendously in the community here

speaker: HS
words: through financing and one thing or other. And it was dead for a year except

speaker: HS
words: the troop I had which met in my basement. And Kiwanis had just started in '26

speaker: HS
words: and they needed a boys and girls' program, is one of thebational programs

speaker: HS
words: of Kiwanis. So I was at the meeting and I proposed to them if they wanted to

speaker: HS
words: put scouting back on the map here in Moscow I'd be willing to see what I could

speaker: HS
words: do about it and head it up. Well, they just fell apart like duck soup. And they

speaker: HS
words: says, Well, how much do you think it will cost?" Well, I said,"Well, I think

speaker: HS
words: start out with about two hundred dollars." So they went along weth me and

speaker: HS
words: we used that two hundred dollars to set up five scout troops. We had a hundred

speaker: HS
words: and twenty-five boys in scouting in within, I would say, three months. It

speaker: HS
words: went over so big that the community then went into the Scout Concil which was

speaker: HS
words: in Lewiston and we boy scouted in Moscow ever since.

speaker: SAM
words: Were you all involved in the starting up of Grizzly Camp as a boy scout camp?

speaker: HS
words: Yes, it was Camp Laird first. And then it was later called.

speaker: HS
words: I spent several weeks up there when they had their summer camp.It

speaker: HS
words: was a very satisfactory place and it is today a very good scout camp

speaker: HS
words: for boys.One problem we had in scouting: most boys don't go into

speaker: HS
words: scouting or don't take up scouting after they're fifteen years old.

speaker: HS
words: That meant they had three years of scouting and a lot of 'em didn't

speaker: HS
words: want to quit The young fellow at the university who happened to be

speaker: HS
words: a sea scout and we knew nothing about sea scouting and he came to

speaker: HS
words: me and suggested that we start a sea scout of these older boys 'cause

speaker: HS
words: that takes in boys from fifteen on up.So we immediately organized a

speaker: HS
words: scout troop or a sea scout troop out of these older boys. And they

speaker: HS
words: had, I imagine 20,25 boys in that program,which helped to carry on

speaker: HS
words: their scouting program.He operated for about three years and of

speaker: HS
words: course, he was through at the university and then it was taken up

speaker: HS
words: by somebody else and then it finally petered out for lack of leadership.

speaker: HS
words: But the nice thing about the sea scout troop,they happened to name it

speaker: HS
words: the Sea Scout Sampson,which I was proud of.

speaker: HS
words: I started to say on Kiwanis,it was one thing Kiwanis did too.

speaker: HS
words: There were a number of people in tovm here felt that we were not getting

speaker: HS
words: the boys that ought to be in scouting.Not particularly under privilege,

speaker: HS
words: but boys that thought it was sissy and so on.So I proposed that we

speaker: HS
words: see what we could do and find out who those boys were.So I went to the

speaker: HS
words: superintendent of schools,Fulton Gale, and asked him for the name of

speaker: HS
words: boys that were giving him trouble.These were boys that were in the

speaker: HS
words: seventh and eighth grade and probably freshmen in high school. He gave

speaker: HS
words: me a list of about nine,ten boys that were giving him trouble. So I went

speaker: HS
words: back to the boys and girls committee of Kiwanis and told this. So they

speaker: HS
words: says,"If you want to head it up, why we'll go ahead and back ya."That

speaker: HS
words: was another two hundred dollars. So formed an athletic club and we

speaker: HS
words: had our bylaws. Based pretty much on the scout program . Only worded

speaker: HS
words: different. And I operated that the first year and Kelly Cline, or Hod

speaker: HS
words: Helpert the next year and Kelly Cline the third year.And we had a very

speaker: HS
words: fine baseball team and a basketball team. We played different teams

speaker: HS
words: around in the community and ...

speaker: SAM
words: This group came from the trouble making kids?

speaker: HS
words: I went to these boys that were the trouble makers and I said, I told

speaker: HS
words: 'em what kind of a program we would put up for 'em.They went for it

speaker: HS
words: and I said we need more boys than that. You get more boys to join the

speaker: HS
words: group. Of course, they went out and got their same kind.So we had a

speaker: HS
words: baseball team with a reserve on the bench of about six and basketball

speaker: HS
words: team with reserves and it worked out very fine. I thought it was a mighty

speaker: HS
words: good program and it was something that was suggested to me that we

speaker: HS
words: werent' reaching those kinds of boys.

speaker: SS
words: There's one question that ask people about the old times and today.

speaker: SS
words: How do you think it compares.Looking back, what do you think the best

speaker: SS
words: times were for Moscow?

speaker: HS
words: The best times? Well from a business standpoint, we had what you'd call

speaker: HS
words: a recession now in 1921 and 2. Long in there. And it affected the

speaker: HS
words: whole community because business dropped considerable. Well we got over

speaker: HS
words: that and immediately business went right up in 1929 we had some of

speaker: HS
words: our best business years then. And then of course when the depression

speaker: HS
words: come on in '30,31 and 2 why we all had to take salary cuts, business

speaker: HS
words: dropped almost fifty per cent and sales.And it took us actually up

speaker: HS
words: until World War II before business got back on its feet again and then

speaker: HS
words: of course, things just popped during World War II. Then after World

speaker: HS
words: War II was over business was I think the best business that Moscow

speaker: HS
words: ever had along '48 and 9.In fact the store had its biggest increase

speaker: HS
words: in business during those years when we had another slight recession

speaker: HS
words: when Eisenhower went in. That didn't amount to anything.And then when

speaker: HS
words: Kennedy went in there was slight recession. Then when Johnson went in,

speaker: HS
words: things started picking up again.We had good business during the latter

speaker: HS
words: sixties. I think as a whole, Moscow and Latah County's been very fortunate

speaker: HS
words: through all the business cycles except for one in '93. We've come out

speaker: HS
words: of all of 'em. We had bankruptcy's been few and it's a pretty good place

speaker: HS
words: for business and it still is.

speaker: SAM
words: Looking at social problems that we have today,what do you think about

speaker: SAM
words: what the energy problem means for the future?

speaker: HS
words: Well we know that the world needs agriculture.lt needs wheat and barley

speaker: HS
words: oats, peas and all that. We know that the farmer is not going to be let

speaker: HS
words: down on energy to produce a crop,he's going to be alright. The rest of

speaker: HS
words: us may have to tighten up and do a little more walking, probably ride

speaker: HS
words: a bicycle and cut our heat down a little.Not waste as much as we're doing.

speaker: HS
words: Probably raise more garden stuff. I think all those things, we're going

speaker: HS
words: to have to do in the next few years.If we're going to come out alive

speaker: HS
words: on this thing. Cause really I think we're headed for a real depression.

speaker: HS
words: This recession they call now is really the begining of it.Usually those

speaker: HS
words: things start in the fall of the year. Stock market goes down in the

speaker: HS
words: fall. And economists don't agree. Some of 'em say we're going to have it

speaker: HS
words: some say we're not. Some say it's going to level off. And others say

speaker: HS
words: we need a depression to get people back to their senses.Just like

speaker: HS
words: you were saying, people drive 55 miles an hour they forgot about

speaker: HS
words: that, a lot of ?em now.They're driving 70 and 75. Just because we've

speaker: HS
words: got plenty of it now.

speaker: SAM
words: Do you think economic problems are recent or long standing?

speaker: HS
words: They've been building up for years. They've been building up ever since

speaker: HS
words: 1932 when Roosevelt went in.He started things in the first place. He

speaker: HS
words: saved a lot of businesses when he put a mordtorium on the banks and

speaker: HS
words: mortgages and one thing or another. He didn't solve the problem

speaker: HS
words: entirely. Maybe it's something that's going to happen every generation.

speaker: HS
words: As far as right now is concerned,I think our federal government is

speaker: HS
words: resbonsible for deflation. If we have a depression they're responsible

speaker: HS
words: for it. Probably more so than the people themselves, because they've

speaker: HS
words: encouraged them.To spend more money, accept federal money. Look at

speaker: HS
words: the programs they've got today. If you build a courthouse, we'll

speaker: HS
words: furnish 70% of it. Or if you build a new schoolbuilding.There's a

speaker: HS
words: program for that.The government's just overspending themselves and

speaker: HS
words: they're forcing it on the people. The people could get along without

speaker: HS
words: that if they wanted to.Now that it's the common; thing to run to the

speaker: HS
words: federal government to back you on just those things.

speaker: SAM
words: What do you think is the role of business in problems of the economy?

speaker: HS
words: Well they say that when you put controls on, people try to work around

speaker: HS
words: them. Its just like prohibition days.When they.cut out liquor, why

speaker: HS
words: you had the bootlegger.And its no different today. People themselves

speaker: HS
words: have got to have a different attitude towards the whole problem rather

speaker: HS
words: than live today and let tomorrow take care of itself.
